attachment.content:
- optimizer: Read if asked to read the optimizer skill
- shepherd: How to organize a population of search agents
- explorer: Read if asked to read the explorer skill
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /agent-home/.claude/skills/explorer

You are a researcher working on an open research problem. The user might prompt you with additional context or instructions. It's important you take them into consideration. If you miss any step or do not follow its rules, your response will be rejected.

**STEP 1: Create your assigned git worktree in `.worktrees/`**
- Create a new git worktree for your assigned branch at `.worktrees/<assigned-branch>`.
- This worktree is your only working directory.
- After creating it, switch into that directory and perform all subsequent work there.
- Use only `git worktree add .worktrees/<assigned-branch> <assigned-branch>`. If it fails, stop.

IMPORTANT GUARDRAIL: Only use files already in your assigned worktree. Do not inspect or copy from `master`, `main`, other `researcher-*` branches, other worktrees, commits, refs, or `.git`. Do not run git history/ref commands like `git log`, `git show`, `git branch`, `git reflog`, `git for-each-ref`, `git checkout <ref> -- ...`, or `git restore --source <ref> ...`.

**STEP 2: Read prompt.md for the problem description, findings.md (if it exists), and any initial programs in your branch**
Pause and reflect on existing findings, if any. Come up with a fundamentally new idea for an improved solution. Do NOT make small, incremental tweaks. 

**STEP 3: Implement and evaluate one idea**
- Focus on one idea. Do not branch out to other major ideas, stay focused on implementing a single idea. There will be future chances to iterate. 
- Use the standard evaluator for evaluating your solution
- After *every* evaluation, EXPLICITLY DECIDE whether to continue iterating or finish up. We have limited budget so be conservative with your iterations. If you have fully implemented a single idea, then finish up. Don't branch out into other ideas that majorly deviate from the initial idea. If the result suggests a targeted fix or improvement, you may continue editing initial_program.py and run ./task-eval again. If the idea doesn't work after a few attempts, give up immediately. Finish up by creating/editing findings.md as needed and committing your changes."

**Step 4: Create/edit findings.md as needed**
- `findings.md` concisely records failures and successes. It is strictly factual and neutral. 
- Only make edits if you learned new facts. Otherwise, keep as is.

IMPORTANT GUARDRAIL: Do NOT discuss next steps or suggestions on what is necessary to improve the score. Simply record the basic facts of your solution and its evaluation. 

**Step 5: Commit all your changes**
- Commit message should follow this format: `{concise experiment description} | score = {score from standard evaluator or concise failure reason}`. 
  - Do not include a score on failure. Only include a description of the failure reason.

When returning a summary back to the orchestrator, include your commit emssage and the commit hash.

content.content: 1 You are a world-class algorithm engineer, and you are very good at programming. Now, you are participating in a programming contest. You are asked to solve a heuristic problem, known as an NP-hard problem. 2 3 Story 4 -------- 5 AtCoder has $n$ cardboard boxes in a warehouse, which are divided into $m$ vertical stacks. 6 Each box is labeled with a unique number from $1,\cdots,n$, and CEO Takahashi wants to carry them out of the warehouse one by one in ascending order of their numbers. 7 In order to carry out a box, he needs to move all the boxes on top of it to another stack. 8 As Takahashi is a very strong man, he can lift and move as many boxes in a stack at a time, but he expends energy depending on the number of boxes he lifts. 9 Please find a way to carry the boxes out that expends as little energy as possible. 10 11 Problem Statement 12 -------- 13 There are $n$ cardboard boxes, each labeled with a unique number from $1,\cdots,n$, divided into $m$ stacks. 14 We refer to the box labeled with the number $v(1\leq v\leq n)$ as "box $v$" and the $i(1\leq i\leq m)$-th stack from the left as "stack $i$". 15 The number of stacks $m$ is a divisor of $n$, and each stack $i$ contains $n/m$ boxes, with numbers $b_{i,1},b_{i,2},\cdots,b_{i,n/m}$ from bottom to top. 16 17 You can repeat the following two types of operations up to $5000$ times. 18 19 1. Choose one box $v (1\leq v\leq n)$ that has not yet been carried out. Remove box $v$ and all boxes above it from the current stack and move them to the top of another stack $i(1\leq i\leq m)$ in the same order. Assume that in the stack $i'$ to which box $v$ belongs, the boxes are numbered $b_{i',1}, \cdots, b_{i',h'}$ from bottom to top, with $b_{i',j} = v$. Also, assume that the boxes in the destination stack $i$ are numbered $b_{i,1}, \cdots, b_{i,h}$ from bottom to top. After this operation, stack $i'$ will become $b_{i',1}, \cdots, b_{i',j-1}$, and stack $i$ will become $b_{i,1}, \cdots, b_{i,h}, b_{i',j}, \cdots, b_{i',h'}$. Let the number of boxes moved by this operation be $k = h' - j + 1$. Then, $k+1$ units of energy will be expended. If $i=i'$, this operation changes nothing and just wastes energy. 20 2. If the smallest number among all the remaining boxes is $v$, and box $v$ is at the top of a stack, then box $v$ can be carried out. This operation does not expend energy. 21 22 Operation 1 cannot create a new stack $i>m$, but it can move boxes into an empty stack $i(1\leq i\leq m)$ after all boxes have been carried out from it by operation 2. 23 24 Please find a sequence of operations that carries out all the boxes with as little total energy expenditure as possible. 25 26 Scoring 27 -------- 28 If all the boxes are carried out with less or equal to $5000$ operations, and the total energy expenditure is $V$, you will obtain a score of $\max(1, 10000-V)$. 29 If you failed to carry out all the boxes, or if you output an illegal operation sequence (specifying out-of-range values or a box that has already been carried out, or specifying a box that does not satisfy the condition in operation 2), it is judged as WA. 30 31 There are $150$ test cases, and the score of a submission is the total score for each test case. 32 If your submission produces an illegal output or exceeds the time limit for some test cases, the submission itself will be judged as WA or TLE , and the score of the submission will be zero. 33 The highest score obtained during the contest will determine the final ranking, and there will be no system test after the contest. 34 If more than one participant gets the same score, they will be ranked in the same place regardless of the submission time. 35 36 37 38 Input 39 -------- 40 Input is given from Standard Input in the following format: 41 ~~~ 42 $n$ $m$ 43 $b_{1,1}$ $\cdots$ $b_{1,n/m}$ 44 $\vdots$ 45 $b_{m,1}$ $\cdots$ $b_{m,n/m}$ 46 ~~~ 47 48 The number of boxes $n$ and the number of stacks $m$ are fixed at $n=200$ and $m=10$ in all the test cases. 49 The number $b_{i,j}$ represents the number of the $j$-th box from the bottom of stack $i$, and satisfies $1\leq b_{i,j}\leq n$. 50 51 Output 52 -------- 53 Let the $k$-th operation be represented by the two integers $(v_k,i_k)$ as follows. 54 55 1. If you use operation 1 to move box $v(1\leq v\leq n)$ and all the boxes stacked on it to another stack $i(1\leq i\leq m)$, then $(v_k,i_k)=(v,i)$. 56 2. If you use operation 2 to carry out box $v$, $(v_k,i_k)=(v,0)$. 57 58 Output the obtained sequence of operations $(v_1,i_1),\cdots,(v_K,i_K)$ ($K\leq 5000$) to Standard Output in the following format: 59 ~~~ 60 $v_1$ $i_1$ 61 $\vdots$ 62 $v_K$ $i_K$ 63 ~~~ 64 65 Show example 66 67 68 Input Generation 69 -------- 70 The box numbers are generated by randomly shuffling the integers from $1$ to $n$ and then dividing them into groups of $n/m$ each. 71 72 Tools (Input generator and visualizer) 73 -------- 74 - Web version: This is more powerful than the local version providing animations. 75 - Local version: You need a compilation environment of Rust language. 76 - Pre-compiled binary for Windows: If you are not familiar with the Rust language environment, please use this instead. 77 78 Please be aware that sharing visualization results or discussing solutions/ideas during the contest is prohibited. 79 80 81 Problem constraints: 82 time_limit=2.0 memory_limit=1073741824 83 84 Evaluation: 85 - Run `./task-eval` 86 - The evaluation timeout is 10000 seconds

file.content: You are a world-class algorithm engineer, and you are very good at programming. Now, you are participating in a programming contest. You are asked to solve a heuristic problem, known as an NP-hard problem. Story -------- AtCoder has $n$ cardboard boxes in a warehouse, which are divided into $m$ vertical stacks. Each box is labeled with a unique number from $1,\cdots,n$, and CEO Takahashi wants to carry them out of the warehouse one by one in ascending order of their numbers. In order to carry out a box, he needs to move all the boxes on top of it to another stack. As Takahashi is a very strong man, he can lift and move as many boxes in a stack at a time, but he expends energy depending on the number of boxes he lifts. Please find a way to carry the boxes out that expends as little energy as possible. Problem Statement -------- There are $n$ cardboard boxes, each labeled with a unique number from $1,\cdots,n$, divided into $m$ stacks. We refer to the box labeled with the number $v(1\leq v\leq n)$ as "box $v$" and the $i(1\leq i\leq m)$-th stack from the left as "stack $i$". The number of stacks $m$ is a divisor of $n$, and each stack $i$ contains $n/m$ boxes, with numbers $b_{i,1},b_{i,2},\cdots,b_{i,n/m}$ from bottom to top. You can repeat the following two types of operations up to $5000$ times. 1. Choose one box $v (1\leq v\leq n)$ that has not yet been carried out. Remove box $v$ and all boxes above it from the current stack and move them to the top of another stack $i(1\leq i\leq m)$ in the same order. Assume that in the stack $i'$ to which box $v$ belongs, the boxes are numbered $b_{i',1}, \cdots, b_{i',h'}$ from bottom to top, with $b_{i',j} = v$. Also, assume that the boxes in the destination stack $i$ are numbered $b_{i,1}, \cdots, b_{i,h}$ from bottom to top. After this operation, stack $i'$ will become $b_{i',1}, \cdots, b_{i',j-1}$, and stack $i$ will become $b_{i,1}, \cdots, b_{i,h}, b_{i',j}, \cdots, b_{i',h'}$. Let the number of boxes moved by this operation be $k = h' - j + 1$. Then, $k+1$ units of energy will be expended. If $i=i'$, this operation changes nothing and just wastes energy. 2. If the smallest number among all the remaining boxes is $v$, and box $v$ is at the top of a stack, then box $v$ can be carried out. This operation does not expend energy. Operation 1 cannot create a new stack $i>m$, but it can move boxes into an empty stack $i(1\leq i\leq m)$ after all boxes have been carried out from it by operation 2. Please find a sequence of operations that carries out all the boxes with as little total energy expenditure as possible. Scoring -------- If all the boxes are carried out with less or equal to $5000$ operations, and the total energy expenditure is $V$, you will obtain a score of $\max(1, 10000-V)$. If you failed to carry out all the boxes, or if you output an illegal operation sequence (specifying out-of-range values or a box that has already been carried out, or specifying a box that does not satisfy the condition in operation 2), it is judged as WA. There are $150$ test cases, and the score of a submission is the total score for each test case. If your submission produces an illegal output or exceeds the time limit for some test cases, the submission itself will be judged as WA or TLE , and the score of the submission will be zero. The highest score obtained during the contest will determine the final ranking, and there will be no system test after the contest. If more than one participant gets the same score, they will be ranked in the same place regardless of the submission time. Input -------- Input is given from Standard Input in the following format: ~~~ $n$ $m$ $b_{1,1}$ $\cdots$ $b_{1,n/m}$ $\vdots$ $b_{m,1}$ $\cdots$ $b_{m,n/m}$ ~~~ The number of boxes $n$ and the number of stacks $m$ are fixed at $n=200$ and $m=10$ in all the test cases. The number $b_{i,j}$ represents the number of the $j$-th box from the bottom of stack $i$, and satisfies $1\leq b_{i,j}\leq n$. Output -------- Let the $k$-th operation be represented by the two integers $(v_k,i_k)$ as follows. 1. If you use operation 1 to move box $v(1\leq v\leq n)$ and all the boxes stacked on it to another stack $i(1\leq i\leq m)$, then $(v_k,i_k)=(v,i)$. 2. If you use operation 2 to carry out box $v$, $(v_k,i_k)=(v,0)$. Output the obtained sequence of operations $(v_1,i_1),\cdots,(v_K,i_K)$ ($K\leq 5000$) to Standard Output in the following format: ~~~ $v_1$ $i_1$ $\vdots$ $v_K$ $i_K$ ~~~ Show example Input Generation -------- The box numbers are generated by randomly shuffling the integers from $1$ to $n$ and then dividing them into groups of $n/m$ each. Tools (Input generator and visualizer) -------- - Web version: This is more powerful than the local version providing animations. - Local version: You need a compilation environment of Rust language. - Pre-compiled binary for Windows: If you are not familiar with the Rust language environment, please use this instead. Please be aware that sharing visualization results or discussing solutions/ideas during the contest is prohibited. Problem constraints: time_limit=2.0 memory_limit=1073741824 Evaluation: - Run `./task-eval` - The evaluation timeout is 10000 seconds

content.content:
1	# Findings
2	
3	## Approach: Greedy heuristic + Simulated Annealing with conservative temperature
4	
5	### Solution Description
6	- **Initial plan**: Greedy heuristic that scores destination stacks based on:
7	  - Penalty for covering soon-needed boxes (urgency-weighted)
8	  - Penalty for burying soon-needed boxes in the moved block
9	  - Penalty for using empty stacks
10	  - Preference for shorter destination stacks
11	  - Bonus for placing soon-needed boxes on top
12	- **SA optimization**: Single-point and 2-point mutations with cooling schedule T: 8→0.5
13	- **Evaluation**: Full re-simulation per SA iteration (N=200 is fast enough)
14	- **Inner loop optimization**: In-place modify + restore on reject (avoids memcpy), batch of 128 iterations between time checks
15	
16	### Key Finding: SA Temperature Sensitivity
17	- High temperatures (T≥50) or block mutations (len≥3) cause WA on the judge. The likely cause is that accepted bad moves create pathological stack states that trigger memory-related issues or TLE on specific test cases.
18	- Low temperature (T=5 constant) works but under-explores. Cooling from T=8 to T=0.5 provides the best results.
19	- T=10→0.3 scored 9278.52 (worse than T=8→0.5), confirming T=8 is near-optimal initial temperature.
20	- Only 1-2 point mutations are safe. Block mutations of 3+ entries cause WA.
21	
22	### Key Finding: Diminishing Returns on SA Optimizations
23	- Greedy repair mutations (simulate prefix, apply greedy) scored 9279.12 — expensive per-iteration cost reduces total iterations, net negative.
24	- Modified greedy heuristic (different constants, sorted stacking bonus, dynamic empty-stack penalty) scored 9276.0 — original heuristic constants are well-tuned.
25	- Global state copy (avoiding vector init) scored 9274.58 — struct copy overhead dominates over vector init savings.
26	- Ensuring mutations always pick different values doesn't help (9278.52).
27	
28	### Key Finding: Beam Search Initial Plan
29	- Beam search (width=12, lookahead=8, 0.4s budget) + SA provides slightly better starting point than pure greedy.
30	- Wider beam (w=20, lookahead=5, 0.6s) scores worse (9280.86) — too much time stolen from SA.
31	
32	### Key Finding: Mutation Bias
33	- Early-biased mutations (quadratic distribution favoring lower indices) improve SA significantly (+4.2 points). Early plan decisions cascade through all subsequent boxes.
34	- Mixed mutations (swap, copy-neighbor) scored 9280.38 — worse than pure random assignment.
35	
36	### Key Finding: Checkpointed Partial Simulation
37	- Attempting to only simulate from the mutation point forward using checkpoints scored 8919.16 — catastrophically worse. The issue is that checkpoints become stale after mutations are accepted, and rebuilding them every 50 accepts still leaves many evaluations using wrong state.
38	
39	### Results
40	| Configuration | Score |
41	|---|---|
42	| Original beam(5) + SA | 9066.78 |
43	| Pure greedy (no SA) | 8799.2 |
44	| Hill climbing only (T=0) | 9117.3 |
45	| SA T=5 constant, 1-point | 9140.84 |
46	| SA T=8→0.5, 1-2 point | 9281.46 |
47	| SA in-place modify+restore, batch 128 | 9282.06 |
48	| Beam(12,8,0.4s) + SA batch 128 | 9283.32 |
49	| Beam(20,5,0.6s) + SA | 9280.86 |
50	| Mixed mutations (swap/copy) | 9280.38 |
51	| Checkpointed partial simulation | 8919.16 |
52	| Beam(12,8) + SA batch=256, early-biased mutations | **9287.52** |
53

file.content:
# Findings

## Approach: Greedy heuristic + Simulated Annealing with conservative temperature

### Solution Description
- **Initial plan**: Greedy heuristic that scores destination stacks based on:
  - Penalty for covering soon-needed boxes (urgency-weighted)
  - Penalty for burying soon-needed boxes in the moved block
  - Penalty for using empty stacks
  - Preference for shorter destination stacks
  - Bonus for placing soon-needed boxes on top
- **SA optimization**: Single-point and 2-point mutations with cooling schedule T: 8→0.5
- **Evaluation**: Full re-simulation per SA iteration (N=200 is fast enough)
- **Inner loop optimization**: In-place modify + restore on reject (avoids memcpy), batch of 128 iterations between time checks

### Key Finding: SA Temperature Sensitivity
- High temperatures (T≥50) or block mutations (len≥3) cause WA on the judge. The likely cause is that accepted bad moves create pathological stack states that trigger memory-related issues or TLE on specific test cases.
- Low temperature (T=5 constant) works but under-explores. Cooling from T=8 to T=0.5 provides the best results.
- T=10→0.3 scored 9278.52 (worse than T=8→0.5), confirming T=8 is near-optimal initial temperature.
- Only 1-2 point mutations are safe. Block mutations of 3+ entries cause WA.

### Key Finding: Diminishing Returns on SA Optimizations
- Greedy repair mutations (simulate prefix, apply greedy) scored 9279.12 — expensive per-iteration cost reduces total iterations, net negative.
- Modified greedy heuristic (different constants, sorted stacking bonus, dynamic empty-stack penalty) scored 9276.0 — original heuristic constants are well-tuned.
- Global state copy (avoiding vector init) scored 9274.58 — struct copy overhead dominates over vector init savings.
- Ensuring mutations always pick different values doesn't help (9278.52).

### Key Finding: Beam Search Initial Plan
- Beam search (width=12, lookahead=8, 0.4s budget) + SA provides slightly better starting point than pure greedy.
- Wider beam (w=20, lookahead=5, 0.6s) scores worse (9280.86) — too much time stolen from SA.

### Key Finding: Mutation Bias
- Early-biased mutations (quadratic distribution favoring lower indices) improve SA significantly (+4.2 points). Early plan decisions cascade through all subsequent boxes.
- Mixed mutations (swap, copy-neighbor) scored 9280.38 — worse than pure random assignment.

### Key Finding: Checkpointed Partial Simulation
- Attempting to only simulate from the mutation point forward using checkpoints scored 8919.16 — catastrophically worse. The issue is that checkpoints become stale after mutations are accepted, and rebuilding them every 50 accepts still leaves many evaluations using wrong state.

### Results
| Configuration | Score |
|---|---|
| Original beam(5) + SA | 9066.78 |
| Pure greedy (no SA) | 8799.2 |
| Hill climbing only (T=0) | 9117.3 |
| SA T=5 constant, 1-point | 9140.84 |
| SA T=8→0.5, 1-2 point | 9281.46 |
| SA in-place modify+restore, batch 128 | 9282.06 |
| Beam(12,8,0.4s) + SA batch 128 | 9283.32 |
| Beam(20,5,0.6s) + SA | 9280.86 |
| Mixed mutations (swap/copy) | 9280.38 |
| Checkpointed partial simulation | 8919.16 |
| Beam(12,8) + SA batch=256, early-biased mutations | **9287.52** |

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the current situation:

1. The current best score is 9287.52/10000 using beam search (width 12) + SA with early-biased mutations.
2. The problem is about sorting boxes from stacks by moving them optimally.
3. The score is 10000 - V where V is total energy. So we need to minimize energy.
4. Each move of k boxes costs k+1 energy.

The user's guidance says: "Try exploring whether a completely different representation or multi-move/split neighborhood could break through the 9290 barrier."

Let me think about what could be fundamentally different:


The current approach uses a simple plan array where each box has a single destination stack, and simulated annealing mutates this plan and re-simulates from scratch. But what if instead of committing each block to one destination, I allowed splitting blocks into multiple sub-blocks that go to different stacks? When uncovering a box, I could move some boxes to one stack and others to another, potentially finding more efficient paths through the state space.

Actually, rereading the operation rules more carefully—when I choose a box, I move it and everything above it as a contiguous block. So I could make multiple moves before carrying out a target box: first move boxes from a higher position to one stack, then move the remaining boxes above my target to another stack, giving me more flexibility in how I arrange things.

The tradeoff is that splitting a group of n boxes into separate moves costs an extra operation per split compared to moving them all at once, but this could pay off by creating better positions for future operations. I'm also realizing I could make strategic pre-emptive moves before I'm forced to carry out a box—rearranging the stacks proactively rather than only moving when necessary.

For the simulated annealing approach, I could explore different neighborhood moves beyond just changing a single box's destination. Splitting a move into multiple destinations, reordering where consecutive boxes go, or mutating a chain of connected boxes could all help escape local optima. The split approach seems most promising since it lets me avoid dumping all boxes above a target into one stack, which can create pollution. The cost is minimal—just one extra energy unit per split—but the positioning benefits could be substantial.

Given the operation budget of 5000 total moves and only needing around 200 for basic placement, there's plenty of room to experiment with splits. I'm thinking about representing each box with a list of (split_point, destination) pairs, allowing up to 2 splits per box to keep the plan manageable. The simulated annealing can then mutate by changing destinations, adding or removing splits, or adjusting split points.

Now I'm working through the mechanics of splitting more carefully—when I split a stack at position p, the first move takes the upper portion to one destination, then the lower portion moves to another. I need to trace through a concrete example to make sure the indexing works correctly.

Looking at the cost tradeoff: moving everything together costs 6, but splitting it into two moves costs 7 total—one extra unit—though the benefit is that the boxes end up separated, which could lead to much better arrangements downstream. So I'm going to simplify the model by allowing at most one split per box, meaning each box can have at most two destination moves.

The representation I'm settling on tracks three things for each box: a primary destination, a split point indicating how many boxes from the bottom stay together, and a secondary destination for the boxes above the split. When we execute this, we move the top portion first since we can only access from the top of the stack, then move the remaining boxes to their primary destination.

Now I'm considering whether this splitting mechanism actually helps. The real benefit would be if boxes stacked above a given box include some that are needed soon—sending those to a better location could reduce future moves.

Let me think about the operation budget. With 200 boxes, I'd have around 200 carry-out operations, and if each box gets one split that's another 400 moves, totaling around 600 operations well within the 5000 limit. So splits are computationally cheap.

I'll go ahead and implement this. The main changes involve extending the plan representation to track split information and updating the simulated annealing moves to handle the new split operations.

Implementing the changes...

content.text:
Now I understand the problem and the current best approach. The key insight from the parent: beam search + SA with early-biased mutations scores 9287.52. The suggestion is to try a fundamentally different representation.

My idea: **Split moves** - instead of moving all boxes above the target to a single destination, allow splitting them into 2 groups sent to different destinations. This costs 1 extra energy per split but can provide much better future positioning. The current plan representation is `dest[k]` for each box k; I'll extend it to optionally split blocks.

Let me implement this.

+    int dest2[MAX_N];     // secondary destination (for top portion when split > 0)
+                        const Plan& plan, int N, int M) {
+                // Split: first move top portion (num_on_top - s boxes) to dest2
+                // Then move bottom portion (s boxes) to dest

content.text:
Excellent! The split-move approach scored **9346.84**, a major improvement over the parent's 9287.52 (a gain of ~59 points). The idea of splitting blocks into two destinations during SA optimization clearly breaks through the 9290 barrier.

Let me check if there's a quick targeted improvement. The batch size is 128 (reduced from 256 in the parent) because simulate_plan with splits is slightly more expensive. Let me see if I can tune the split probability or batch size slightly. But given the instruction to be conservative, this is already a solid result. Let me also check the operation count concern - splits add more operations which could push near the 5000 limit on some cases.

Actually, looking more carefully, the split adds an extra operation (2 moves instead of 1), so on cases where there are many boxes to move, we could hit the 5000 operation limit. Let me add a safeguard by checking operation count during simulation and limiting splits. But since the evaluator says ACCEPTED on all test cases, this isn't causing issues now.

The score is very strong (+59 points improvement). Let me finalize and commit.

### Key Finding: Split-Move Neighborhood
- Extended plan representation to allow splitting a block into two portions sent to different destinations. Each split costs one extra move operation but enables much better future positioning.
- SA mutation distribution: 50% dest mutation, 20% toggle split, 15% change split point, 10% change dest2, 5% 2-point dest mutation. All early-biased (quadratic).
- Split point is random 1..15 (clamped to actual block size during simulation).
- Batch size reduced to 128 due to slightly higher cost per simulate_plan call.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Findings

## Approach: Greedy heuristic + Simulated Annealing with conservative temperature

### Solution Description
- **Initial plan**: Greedy heuristic that scores destination stacks based on:
  - Penalty for covering soon-needed boxes (urgency-weighted)
  - Penalty for burying soon-needed boxes in the moved block
  - Penalty for using empty stacks
  - Preference for shorter destination stacks
  - Bonus for placing soon-needed boxes on top
- **SA optimization**: Single-point and 2-point mutations with cooling schedule T: 8→0.5
- **Evaluation**: Full re-simulation per SA iteration (N=200 is fast enough)
- **Inner loop optimization**: In-place modify + restore on reject (avoids memcpy), batch of 128 iterations between time checks

### Key Finding: SA Temperature Sensitivity
- High temperatures (T≥50) or block mutations (len≥3) cause WA on the judge. The likely cause is that accepted bad moves create pathological stack states that trigger memory-related issues or TLE on specific test cases.
- Low temperature (T=5 constant) works but under-explores. Cooling from T=8 to T=0.5 provides the best results.
- T=10→0.3 scored 9278.52 (worse than T=8→0.5), confirming T=8 is near-optimal initial temperature.
- Only 1-2 point mutations are safe. Block mutations of 3+ entries cause WA.

### Key Finding: Diminishing Returns on SA Optimizations
- Greedy repair mutations (simulate prefix, apply greedy) scored 9279.12 — expensive per-iteration cost reduces total iterations, net negative.
- Modified greedy heuristic (different constants, sorted stacking bonus, dynamic empty-stack penalty) scored 9276.0 — original heuristic constants are well-tuned.
- Global state copy (avoiding vector init) scored 9274.58 — struct copy overhead dominates over vector init savings.
- Ensuring mutations always pick different values doesn't help (9278.52).

### Key Finding: Beam Search Initial Plan
- Beam search (width=12, lookahead=8, 0.4s budget) + SA provides slightly better starting point than pure greedy.
- Wider beam (w=20, lookahead=5, 0.6s) scores worse (9280.86) — too much time stolen from SA.

### Key Finding: Mutation Bias
- Early-biased mutations (quadratic distribution favoring lower indices) improve SA significantly (+4.2 points). Early plan decisions cascade through all subsequent boxes.
- Mixed mutations (swap, copy-neighbor) scored 9280.38 — worse than pure random assignment.

### Key Finding: Checkpointed Partial Simulation
- Attempting to only simulate from the mutation point forward using checkpoints scored 8919.16 — catastrophically worse. The issue is that checkpoints become stale after mutations are accepted, and rebuilding them every 50 accepts still leaves many evaluations using wrong state.

### Results
| Configuration | Score |
|---|---|
| Original beam(5) + SA | 9066.78 |
| Pure greedy (no SA) | 8799.2 |
| Hill climbing only (T=0) | 9117.3 |
| SA T=5 constant, 1-point | 9140.84 |
| SA T=8→0.5, 1-2 point | 9281.46 |
| SA in-place modify+restore, batch 128 | 9282.06 |
| Beam(12,8,0.4s) + SA batch 128 | 9283.32 |
| Beam(20,5,0.6s) + SA | 9280.86 |
| Mixed mutations (swap/copy) | 9280.38 |
| Checkpointed partial simulation | 8919.16 |
| Beam(12,8) + SA batch=256, early-biased mutations | **9287.52** |

lines: - Attempting to only simulate from the mutation point forward using checkpoints scored 8919.16 — catastrophically worse. The issue is that checkpoints become stale after mutations are accepted, and rebuilding them every 50 accepts still leaves many evaluations using wrong state.
+- Extended plan representation to allow splitting a block into two portions sent to different destinations. Each split costs one extra move operation but enables much better future positioning.
+- SA mutation distribution: 50% dest mutation, 20% toggle split, 15% change split point, 10% change dest2, 5% 2-point dest mutation. All early-biased (quadratic).
+- Split point is random 1..15 (clamped to actual block size during simulation).
+- Batch size reduced to 128 due to slightly higher cost per simulate_plan call.

content.text:
**Summary:**

Commit: `9a2eb1e` — `Split-move neighborhood in SA: allow splitting blocks to 2 destinations | score = 9346.84`

The key idea: instead of moving all boxes above the target to a single destination, the SA can now optionally **split the block into two portions** sent to different destinations. This costs one extra move operation per split but enables much better future positioning of boxes. The extended plan representation adds `split[k]` (split point) and `dest2[k]` (second destination) per box, with SA mutations covering: dest change (50%), toggle split (20%), change split point (15%), change dest2 (10%), 2-point dest (5%). Score improved from 9287.52 to **9346.84** (+59 points).
